explanation.sourceText: The English verb "have" originates from Old English "habban", meaning "to hold, possess", with related forms in other Germanic languages.
explanation.targetText: Cuvântul englezesc „have“ provine din engleza veche „habban“, care înseamnă „a ține, a poseda“, cu forme înrudită în alte limbi germanice.

source.additional: Have is an irregular verb used both as a main verb and as an auxiliary verb in various tenses.

target.definition: a fi în posesia a ceva

explanation.sourceText: The Romanian verb "a avea" derives from the Latin "habere", meaning "to have" or "to possess", associated with the concept of ownership and care.
explanation.targetText: Verbul românesc „a avea“ provine din latină „habere“, care înseamnă „a avea, a poseda“.

target.additional: An irregular verb appearing in many conjugated forms and often used as an auxiliary verb.

explanation.sourceText: "Have" refers to the possession of things or rights.
explanation.targetText: 'A avea' se referă la posesia unor obiecte sau drepturi.

explanation.sourceText: "Have" is also used as an auxiliary verb in the past tense.
explanation.targetText: 'A avea' este folosit și ca verb auxiliar la timpul trecut.

learningTips.text: Remember that "a avea" means "to have" or "to possess" in Romanian. It’s often used as an auxiliary verb, just like in English.
Think of the English word "have" as holding something in your hands—this helps connect the meaning of possession.
Practice common phrases like "Eu am" (I have), "Eu am avut" (I had), and "Eu voi avea" (I will have) to get comfortable with different tenses.
Link the Romanian "a avea" with the English "have"—they sound similar and share the same Latin root, making it easier to remember.
